Tier 2: laravel-lang-supply-chain-attack-credential-stealer-may-2026 (Global) — over May 22-23 an attacker rewrote ~700 existing Git release tags across the Laravel-Lang PHP localization packages (laravel-lang/lang, http-statuses, attributes, etc.) on Packagist to point at a malicious commit in an attacker fork, so even version-pinned projects pulled poisoned code; injected helpers.php wired into Composer autoload.files runs on every request the moment the package installs, fetches a cross-platform second stage, and runs a credential stealer (cloud keys, CI/CD tokens, SSH, .env, browser/password-manager data, crypto wallets). Packagist pulled and unlisted the affected versions. Verified across The Hacker News, BleepingComputer, StepSecurity, Aikido, Snyk, Mend, gbhackers, plus the upstream GitHub issue (Laravel-Lang/common#257). Chosen despite being PHP/Composer (outside Gabe's stack) because (a) it is the weekend's one genuinely fresh + uncovered dev-security event, (b) it maps directly onto Gabe's own `pip install` workflow (install-time code execution; version tags aren't safe pins; use lockfiles + hash verification), and (c) it has a real, well-documented AI angle — 2026 research shows AI coding agents auto-install dependencies, pick known-vulnerable versions ~50% more often than humans, and reference non-existent packages ~20% of the time (slopsquatting), so an agent running installs unattended removes the human review step this attack is built to slip past; the systemic AI-agent supply-chain research (CSO Online, Security Boulevard Mar 2026, a16z 'Et Tu, Agent?', the arXiv dependency-selection study dated Dec 2025) was folded into why_it_matters + a CSO Online take rather than made a separate item, since those analysis pieces are months old and not a fresh 'today' event.
